[How to Configure Azure Disk CSI Driver for AKS Persistent Volumes](https://oneuptime.com/blog/post/2026-02-09-azure-disk-csi-driver-aks/view)\n\n# Azure Disk CSI Driver Helm Chart Installation for Self-Managed k3s\n\n## Installation Method\n\nThe recommended way to install the Azure Disk CSI driver is via Helm.[1] Here are the steps:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n# Add the Azure Disk CSI Helm repository\nhelm repo add azuredisk-csi-driver https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes-sigs/azuredisk-csi-driver/master/charts\nhelm repo update\n\n# Install the driver\nhelm install azuredisk-csi-driver azuredisk-csi-driver/azuredisk-csi-driver \\\n  --namespace kube-system \\\n  --set controller.replicas=2 \\\n  --set cloud=AzurePublicCloud \\\n  --set linux.distro=fedora\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n**Note on distro setting:** The &#96;linux.distro=fedora&#96; parameter is important for non-standard filesystem layouts to avoid mount path detection issues.[1]\n\n## Prerequisites\n\nBefore installation, ensure you have:[1]\n- A service principal or managed identity with disk management permissions\n- &#96;kubectl&#96; and Helm installed\n- The Azure cloud provider configured and running\n- A cloud provider config file at &#96;/etc/kubernetes/azure.json&#96; (or configured via ConfigMap)[3]\n\n## IAM Permissions\n\nThe CSI driver requires permissions to manage Azure Managed Disks. Minimum required actions include:[1]\n- &#96;Microsoft.Compute/disks/read&#96;, &#96;write&#96;, &#96;delete&#96;\n- &#96;Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/read&#96;, &#96;write&#96;\n- &#96;Microsoft.Compute/snapshots/read&#96;, &#96;write&#96;, &#96;delete&#96;\n\n## Verification\n\nAfter deployment, verify the installation:[1]\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\nkubectl get pods -n kube-system -l app=csi-azuredisk-controller\nkubectl get pods -n kube-system -l app=csi-azuredisk-node\nkubectl get csidrivers | grep disk.csi.azure.com\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Kubernetes Compatibility\n\nThe latest driver version (v1.34.2 as of March 2026) supports Kubernetes 1.21+.[3]",

[GitHub - kubernetes-sigs/azuredisk-csi-driver: Azure Disk CSI Driver · GitHub](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/azuredisk-csi-driver/tree/master)\n\n# Azure Disk CSI Driver: Service Principal Configuration for Non-AKS Kubernetes\n\n## Service Principal Setup\n\nTo use service principal credentials with the Azure Disk CSI driver on non-AKS Kubernetes clusters, you need to configure service principal authentication. [2]\n\n**Key Requirements:**\n- The service principal must have an &#96;appId&#96; (Client ID) and &#96;password&#96; (Client Secret)[2]\n- The service principal requires appropriate permissions to access Azure Key Vault and Azure Disk resources[2]\n\n**Configuration Method:**\nService principal credentials should be added as Kubernetes secrets that are accessible by the CSI driver. For pod-level access using service principal mode, include a &#96;nodePublishSecretRef&#96; in your volume configuration that references a secret containing the credentials.[2]\n\n## Helm Chart Values\n\nThe azure-disk-csi-driver provides Helm charts for deployment. [3][4] Multiple chart versions are available in the repository's &#96;/charts&#96; directory, with the latest version in the &#96;latest&#96; folder.[3]\n\n**Important Note:** \nDeploying this driver manually outside of AKS is not an officially supported Microsoft product. For a fully managed and supported experience on Kubernetes, Microsoft recommends using AKS with the managed Azure Disk CSI driver.[4]\n\n## Prerequisites for Non-AKS Clusters\n\nThe driver requires a cloud provider config file at specific paths depending on your platform (e.g., &#96;/etc/kubernetes/azure.json&#96; for standard Kubernetes, &#96;/etc/kubernetes/cloud.conf&#96; for Azure Red Hat OpenShift).[4] You can override the config path using a ConfigMap named &#96;azure-cred-file&#96;.[4]",

[Setting up the Azure Cloud Provider](https://documentation.suse.com/cloudnative/rancher-manager/v2.10/en/cluster-deployment/set-up-cloud-providers/azure.html)\n4. [Setting Up the Azure Cloud Provider for RKE2 // Support Tools](https://support.tools/training/rke2/azure-cloud-provider-rke2/)\n5. [Setting up the Azure Cloud Provider | Rancher](https://ranchermanager.docs.rancher.com/how-to-guides/new-user-guides/kubernetes-clusters-in-rancher-setup/set-up-cloud-providers/azure)\n\n# Azure.json Cloud Config File Format for K3s Azure Disk CSI Driver\n\n## File Structure\n\nThe &#96;azure.json&#96; cloud configuration file uses JSON format with the following required authentication fields:[1]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;json\n{\n    \"cloud\": \"AzurePublicCloud\",\n    \"tenantId\": \"0000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000\",\n    \"aadClientId\": \"0000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000\",\n    \"aadClientSecret\": \"0000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000\",\n    \"subscriptionId\": \"0000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000\",\n    \"resourceGroup\": \"\u003cname\u003e\",\n    \"location\": \"eastus\",\n    ...\n}\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Required Fields for Azure Disk CSI Driver\n\n- **tenantId**: The AAD Tenant ID for the Azure Subscription where the cluster is deployed (required)[1]\n- **subscriptionId**: The ID of the Azure Subscription where the cluster is deployed (required)[1]\n- **aadClientId**: The ClientID for an AAD application with RBAC access to Azure RM APIs (used for service principal authentication)[1]\n- **aadClientSecret**: The ClientSecret for the AAD application (used for service principal authentication)[1]\n\n## Important Notes\n\nAll values are string type.[1] The file is typically located at &#96;/etc/kubernetes/azure.json&#96; on Kubernetes nodes, though in OpenShift environments it may be located at &#96;/etc/kubernetes/cloud.conf&#96;.[2] The CSI driver can also read the cloud config from Kubernetes secrets serialized as &#96;cloud-config&#96; key in a secret named &#96;azure-cloud-provider&#96; in the &#96;kube-system&#96; namespace.[2]",

[Create and Manage Persistent Volumes with Azure Disks in Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) - Azure Kubernetes Service | Microsoft Learn](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/create-volume-azure-disk)\n2. [Create and Manage Persistent Volumes with Azure Disks in Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) - Azure Kubernetes Service | Microsoft Learn](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/azure-csi-disk-storage-provision?WT.mc_id=devto-blog-abhishgu)\n5. [Use a custom StorageClass with your workloads  |  GKE on Azure  |  Google Cloud Documentation](https://docs.cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/multi-cloud/docs/azure/how-to/storage-class)\n\n# Azure Disk CSI Driver StorageClass Configuration\n\n## CSI Plugin Name and Provisioner\nThe Azure Disk CSI driver uses the provisioner &#96;disk.csi.azure.com&#96;.[3]\n\n## Built-in StorageClasses\nAKS clusters include two built-in StorageClasses configured for Azure Disks:[1][4]\n\n- **managed-csi**: Provisions Standard SSD Azure Disks backed by locally redundant storage (LRS)\n- **managed-csi-premium**: Provisions premium Azure Disks with high-performance SSD\n\n## Custom StorageClass Example\nTo create a custom StorageClass with the Azure Disk CSI driver:[5]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\napiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1\nkind: StorageClass\nmetadata:\n  name: my-custom-class\nprovisioner: disk.csi.azure.com\nvolumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer\nallowVolumeExpansion: true\nparameters:\n  storageAccount: my-storage-account\n  tags: group=dev\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Key Features\n- Supported access mode: &#96;ReadWriteOnce&#96;[3]\n- Zone-redundant storage (ZRS) support available for &#96;Premium_ZRS&#96; and &#96;StandardSSD_ZRS&#96; disk types[2]\n- Premium SSD v1 and v2 support (v2 only supports None caching mode)[2]\n- Performance improvements during concurrent disk attach/detach operations compared to in-tree drivers[2]\n\n## Prerequisites\nThe Azure Disk CSI driver must be enabled on your cluster, and it requires a cloud provider configuration file at &#96;/etc/kubernetes/azure.json&#96; (AKS/CAPZ) or &#96;/etc/kubernetes/cloud.conf&#96; (ARO).[3]",

Default to&#96;AzurePublicCloud&#96;. |\n| tenantID | The AAD Tenant ID for the Subscription that the cluster is deployed in | Required. |\n| aadClientID | The ClientID for an AAD application with RBAC access to talk to Azure RM APIs | Used for service principal authn. |\n| aadClientSecret | The ClientSecret for an AAD application with RBAC access to talk to Azure RM APIs | Used for service principal authn. |\n| aadClientCertPath | The path of a client certificate for an AAD application with RBAC access to talk to Azure RM APIs | Used for client cert authn. |\n| aadClientCertPassword | The password of the client certificate for an AAD application with RBAC access to talk to Azure RM APIs | Used for client cert authn. |\n| useManagedIdentityExtension | Use managed service identity for the virtual machine to access Azure ARM APIs | Boolean type, default to false. |\n| userAssignedIdentityID | The Client ID of the user assigned MSI which is assigned to the underlying VMs | Required for user-assigned managed identity. |\n| subscriptionId | The ID of the Azure Subscription that the cluster is deployed in | Required. |\n| identitySystem | The identity system for AzureStack. Supported values are: ADFS | Only used for AzureStack |\n| networkResourceTenantID | The AAD Tenant ID for the Subscription that the network resources are deployed in | Optional. Supported since v1.18.0. Only used for hosting network resources in different AAD Tenant and Subscription than those for the cluster. |\n| networkResourceSubscriptionID | The ID of the Azure Subscription that the network resources are deployed in | Optional. Supported since v1.18.0. Only used for hosting network resources in different AAD Tenant and Subscription than those for the cluster. |\n\nNote: Cloud provider currently supports three authentication methods, you can choose one combination of them:\n\n[Managed Identity](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/managed-service-identity/overview):\n\n- For system-assigned managed identity: set&#96;useManagedIdentityExtension&#96; to true\n- For user-assigned managed identity: set&#96;useManagedIdentityExtension&#96; to true and also set&#96;userAssignedIdentityID&#96;\n\nIf more than one value is set, the order is&#96;Managed Identity&#96;\u003e&#96;Service Principal&#96;\u003e&#96;Client Certificate&#96;.\n\n## Cluster config\n\n| Name | Description | Remark |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| resourceGroup | The name of the resource group that the cluster is deployed in |\n| location | The location of the resource group that the cluster is deployed in |\n| vnetName | The name of the VNet that the cluster is deployed in |\n| vnetResourceGroup | The name of the resource group that the Vnet is deployed in |\n| subnetName | The name of the subnet that the cluster is deployed in |\n| securityGroupName | The name of the security group attached to the cluster’s subnet |\n| securityGroupResourceGroup | The name of the resource group that the security group is deployed in |\n| routeTableName | The name of the route table attached to the subnet that the cluster is deployed in | Optional in 1.6 |\n| primaryAvailabilitySetName [*](https://cloud-provider-azure.sigs.k8s.io/install/configs/#primaryavailabilitysetname) | The name of the availability set that should be used as the load balancer backend | Optional |\n| vmType | The type of azure nodes. Candidate values are:&#96;vmss&#96;,&#96;vmssflex&#96; and&#96;standard&#96; | Optional, default to&#96;standard&#96; for v1.27 and below versions and&#96;vmss&#96; for v1.28+ versions |\n| primaryScaleSetName [*](https://cloud-provider-azure.sigs.k8s.io/install/configs/#primaryscalesetname) | The name of the scale set that should be used as the load balancer backend | Optional |\n| cloudProviderBackoff | Enable exponential backoff to manage resource request retries | Boolean value, default to false |\n| cloudProviderBackoffRetries | Backoff retry limit | Integer value, valid if&#96;cloudProviderBackoff&#96; is true |\n| cloudProviderBackoffExponent | Backoff exponent | Float value, valid if&#96;cloudProviderBackoff&#96; is true |\n| cloudProviderBackoffDuration | Backoff duration | Integer value, valid if&#96;cloudProviderBackoff&#96; is true |\n| cloudProviderBackoffJitter | Backoff jitter | Float value, valid if&#96;cloudProviderBackoff&#96; is true |\n| cloudProviderBackoffMode | Backoff mode, supported values are “v2” and “default”. Note that “v2” has been deprecated since v1.18.0. | Default to “default” |\n| cloudProviderRateLimit | Enable rate limiting | Boolean value, default to false |\n| cloudProviderRateLimitQPS | Rate limit QPS (Read) | Float value, valid if&#96;cloudProviderRateLimit&#96; is true |\n| cloudProviderRateLimitBucket | Rate limit Bucket Size | Integar value, valid if&#96;cloudProviderRateLimit&#96; is true |\n| cloudProviderRateLimitQPSWrite | Rate limit QPS (Write) | Float value, valid if&#96;cloudProviderRateLimit&#96; is true |\n| cloudProviderRateLimitBucketWrite | Rate limit Bucket Size | Integer value, valid if&#96;cloudProviderRateLimit&#96; is true |\n| useInstanceMetadata | Use instance metadata service where possible | Boolean value, default to false |\n| loadBalancerSku | Sku of Load Balancer and Public IP. Candidate values are:&#96;basic&#96; and&#96;standard&#96;. | Default to&#96;basic&#96;. |\n| excludeMasterFromStandardLB | ExcludeMasterFromStandardLB excludes master nodes from standard load balancer. | Boolean value, default to true. |\n| disableOutboundSNAT | Disable outbound SNAT for SLB | Default to false and available since v1.11.9, v1.12.7, v1.13.5 and v1.14.0 |\n| maximumLoadBalancerRuleCount | Maximum allowed LoadBalancer Rule Count is the limit enforced by Azure Load balancer | Integer value, default to [148](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/v1.10.0/pkg/cloudprovider/providers/azure/azure.go#L48) |\n| routeTableResourceGroup | The resource group name for routeTable | Default same as resourceGroup and available since v1.15.0 |\n| loadBalancerName | Working together with loadBalancerResourceGroup to determine the LB name in a different resource group | Since v1.18.0, default is cluster name setting on kube-controller-manager |\n| loadBalancerResourceGroup | The load balancer resource group name, which is different from node resource group | Since v1.18.0, default is same as resourceGroup |\n| disableAvailabilitySetNodes | Disable supporting for AvailabilitySet virtual machines in vmss cluster. It should only be used when vmType is “vmss” and all the nodes (including master) are VMSS Uniform virtual machines | Since v1.18.0, default is false |\n| enableVmssFlexNodes | Enable supporting for VMSS Flex virtual machines in vmss cluster. It should only be used when vmType is “vmss” | Since v1.26.0, default is false |\n| availabilitySetsCacheTTLInSeconds | Cache TTL in seconds for availabilitySet Nodes | Since v1.18.0, default is 600 |\n| vmssCacheTTLInSeconds | Cache TTL in seconds for VMSS | Since v1.18.0, default is 600 |\n| vmssVirtualMachinesCacheTTLInSeconds | Cache TTL in seconds for VMSS virtual machines | Since v1.18.0, default is 600 |\n| vmCacheTTLInSeconds | Cache TTL in seconds for virtual machines | Since v1.18.0, default is 60 |\n| loadBalancerCacheTTLInSeconds | Cache TTL in seconds for load balancers | Since v1.18.0, default is 120 |\n| nsgCacheTTLInSeconds | Cache TTL in seconds for network security group | Since v1.18.0, default is 120 |\n| routeTableCacheTTLInSeconds | Cache TTL in seconds for route table | Since v1.18.0, default is 120 |\n| disableAzureStackCloud | DisableAzureStackCloud disables AzureStackCloud support. Set to true if you are using AZURESTACKCLOUD environment variable to configure custom endpoints but not actually running on AzureStack. Default is false. |\n| tags | Tags that would be tagged onto the cloud provider managed resources, including lb, public IP, network security group and route table. | Optional. Supported since v1.20.0. |\n| tagsMap | JSON-style tags, will be merged with&#96;tags&#96; | Optional. Supported since v1.23.0. |\n| systemTags | Tag keys that should not be deleted when being updated. | Optional. Supported since v1.21.0. |\n| loadBalancerBackendPoolConfigurationType | The type of the Load Balancer backend pool. Supported values are&#96;nodeIPConfiguration&#96;(default) and&#96;nodeIP&#96; | Optional. Supported since v1.23.0 |\n| putVMSSVMBatchSize | The number of requests the client sends concurrently in a batch when putting the VMSS VMs. Anything smaller than or equal to 0 means to update VMSS VMs one by one in sequence. | Optional. Supported since v1.24.0. |\n| enableMigrateToIPBasedBackendPoolAPI | Use the migration API to migrate from NIC-based to IP-based Load Balancer backend pools without downtime. | Optional. Supported since v1.24.0. |\n| RouteUpdateIntervalInSeconds | Update interval for route updater, default to 30. | Optional. Supported since v1.28.0 |\n| LoadBalancerBackendPoolUpdateIntervalInSeconds | Update interval for local service backend pool updater in multi-slb mode, default to 30. | Optional. Supported since v1.28.0 |\n| multipleStandardLoadBalancerConfigurations | Configurations related to multiple standard load balancers. See [multi-slb documentation](https://cloud-provider-azure.sigs.k8s.io/topics/multislb/) for details. | Optional. Supported since v1.28.0 |\n\n### primaryAvailabilitySetName\n\nIf this is set, the Azure cloudprovider will only add nodes from that availability set to the load balancer backend pool. If this is not set, and multiple agent pools (availability sets) are used, then the cloudprovider will try to add all nodes to a single backend pool which is forbidden. In other words, if you use multiple agent pools (availability sets), you MUST set this field.\n\n### primaryScaleSetName\n\nIf this is set, the Azure cloudprovider will only add nodes from that scale set to the load balancer backend pool. If this is not set, and multiple agent pools (scale sets) are used, then the cloudprovider will try to add all nodes to a single backend pool which is forbidden when using Load Balancer Basic SKU. In other words, if you use multiple agent pools (scale sets), and&#96;loadBalancerSku&#96; is set to&#96;basic&#96; you MUST set this field.\n\n### excludeMasterFromStandardLB\n\nMaster nodes are not added to the backends of Azure Load Balancer (ALB) if&#96;excludeMasterFromStandardLB&#96; is set.\n\nBy default, if nodes are labeled with&#96;node-role.kubernetes.io/master&#96;, they would also be excluded from ALB. If you want to add the master nodes to ALB,&#96;excludeMasterFromStandardLB&#96; should be set to false and label&#96;node-role.kubernetes.io/master&#96; should be removed if it has already been applied.\n\n### Dynamically reloading cloud controller manager\n\nSince v1.21.0, Azure cloud provider supports reading the cloud config from Kubernetes secrets. The secret is a serialized version of&#96;azure.json&#96; file. When the secret is changed, the cloud controller manager will re-constructing itself without restarting the pod.\n\nTo enable this feature, set&#96;--enable-dynamic-reloading=true&#96; and configure the secret name, namespace and data key by&#96;--cloud-config-secret-name&#96;,&#96;--cloud-config-secret-namespace&#96; and&#96;--cloud-config-key&#96;. When initializing from secret, the&#96;--cloud-config&#96; should not be set.\n\nNote that the&#96;--enable-dynamic-reloading&#96; cannot be&#96;false&#96; if&#96;--cloud-config&#96; is empty. To build the cloud provider from classic config file, please explicitly specify the&#96;--cloud-config&#96; and do not set&#96;--enable-dynamic-reloading=true&#96;. In this manner, the cloud controller manager will not be updated when the config file is changed. You need to restart the pod to manually trigger the re-initialization.\n\nSince Azure cloud provider would read Kubernetes secrets, the following RBAC should also be configured:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n---\napiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1beta1\nkind: ClusterRole\nmetadata:\n  labels:\n    kubernetes.io/cluster-service: \"true\"\n  name: system:azure-cloud-provider-secret-getter\nrules:\n- apiGroups: [\"\"]\n  resources: [\"secrets\"]\n  resourceNames: [\"azure-cloud-provider\"]\n  verbs:\n  - get\n---\napiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1beta1\nkind: ClusterRoleBinding\nmetadata:\n  labels:\n    kubernetes.io/cluster-service: \"true\"\n  name: system:azure-cloud-provider-secret-getter\nroleRef:\n  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io\n  kind: ClusterRole\n  name: system:azure-cloud-provider-secret-getter\nsubjects:\n- kind: ServiceAccount\n  name: azure-cloud-provider\n  namespace: kube-system\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nIt is also supported to build the cloud controller manager from the cloud config file and reload dynamically. To use this way, turn on&#96;--enable-dynamic-reloading&#96; and set&#96;--cloud-config&#96; to an non-empty value.\n\n### per client rate limiting\n\nSince v1.18.0, the original global rate limiting has been switched to per-client. A set of new rate limit configure options are introduced for each client, which includes:\n\n- [Client Certificate](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/active-directory-protocols-oauth-service-to-service): set&#96;aadClientCertPath&#96; and&#96;aadClientCertPassword&#96;\n- routeRateLimit\n- SubnetsRateLimit\n- InterfaceRateLimit\n- RouteTableRateLimit\n- LoadBalancerRateLimit\n- PublicIPAddressRateLimit\n- SecurityGroupRateLimit\n- VirtualMachineRateLimit\n- StorageAccountRateLimit\n- DiskRateLimit\n- SnapshotRateLimit\n- VirtualMachineScaleSetRateLimit\n- VirtualMachineSizeRateLimit\n- AvailabilitySetRateLimit\n- AttachDetachDiskRateLimit\n- ContainerServiceRateLimit\n- DeploymentRateLimit\n- PrivateDNSRateLimit\n- PrivateDNSZoneGroupRateLimit\n- PrivateEndpointRateLimit\n- PrivateLinkServiceRateLimit\n- VirtualNetworkRateLimit\n\nThe original rate limiting options (“cloudProviderRateLimitBucket”, “cloudProviderRateLimitBucketWrite”, “cloudProviderRateLimitQPS”, “cloudProviderRateLimitQPSWrite”) are still supported, and they would be the default values if per-client rate limiting is not configured.\n\nHere is an example of per-client config:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n{\n  // default rate limit (enabled).\n  \"cloudProviderRatelimit\": true,\n  \"cloudProviderRateLimitBucket\": 1,\n  \"cloudProviderRateLimitBucketWrite\": 1,\n  \"cloudProviderRateLimitQPS\": 1,\n  \"cloudProviderRateLimitQPSWrite\": 1,\n  \"virtualMachineScaleSetRateLimit\": {  // VMSS specific (enabled).\n    \"cloudProviderRatelimit\": true,\n    \"cloudProviderRateLimitBucket\": 2,\n    \"CloudProviderRateLimitBucketWrite\": 2,\n    \"cloudProviderRateLimitQPS\": 0,\n    \"CloudProviderRateLimitQPSWrite\": 0\n  },\n  \"loadBalancerRateLimit\": {  // LB specific (disabled)\n    \"cloudProviderRatelimit\": false\n  },\n  ... // other cloud provider configs\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Run Kubelet without Azure identity\n\nWhen running Kubelet with kube-controller-manager, it also supports running without Azure identity since v1.15.0.\n\nBoth kube-controller-manager and kubelet should configure&#96;--cloud-provider=azure --cloud-config=/etc/kubernetes/cloud-config/azure.json&#96;, but the contents for&#96;azure.json&#96; are different:\n\n(1) For kube-controller-manager, refer the above part for setting&#96;azure.json&#96;.\n\n(2) For kubelet,&#96;useInstanceMetadata&#96; is required to be&#96;true&#96; and Azure identities are not required. A sample for Kubelet’s azure.json is\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n{\n  \"useInstanceMetadata\": true,\n  \"vmType\": \"vmss\"\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Azure Stack Configuration\n\nAzure Stack has different API endpoints, depending on the Azure Stack deployment. These need to be provided to the Azure SDK and currently this is done by adding an extra&#96;json&#96; file with the arguments, as well as an environment variable pointing to this file.\n\nThere are several available presets, namely:\n\n- &#96;AzureChinaCloud&#96;\n- &#96;AzureGermanCloud&#96;\n- &#96;AzurePublicCloud&#96;\n- &#96;AzureUSGovernmentCloud&#96;\n\nThese are determined using&#96;cloud: &#96; described above in the description of&#96;azure.json&#96;.\n\nWhen&#96;cloud: AzureStackCloud&#96;, the extra environment variable used by the Azure SDK to find the Azure Stack configuration file is:\n\n- [AZURE_ENVIRONMENT_FILEPATH](https://github.com/Azure/go-autorest/blob/562d376/autorest/azure/environments.go#L28)\n\nThe configuration parameters of this file:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n{\n  \"name\": \"AzureStackCloud\",\n  \"managementPortalURL\": \"...\",\n  \"publishSettingsURL\": \"...\",\n  \"serviceManagementEndpoint\": \"...\",\n  \"resourceManagerEndpoint\": \"...\",\n  \"activeDirectoryEndpoint\": \"...\",\n  \"galleryEndpoint\": \"...\",\n  \"keyVaultEndpoint\": \"...\",\n  \"graphEndpoint\": \"...\",\n  \"serviceBusEndpoint\": \"...\",\n  \"batchManagementEndpoint\": \"...\",\n  \"storageEndpointSuffix\": \"...\",\n  \"sqlDatabaseDNSSuffix\": \"...\",\n  \"trafficManagerDNSSuffix\": \"...\",\n  \"keyVaultDNSSuffix\": \"...\",\n  \"serviceBusEndpointSuffix\": \"...\",\n  \"serviceManagementVMDNSSuffix\": \"...\",\n  \"resourceManagerVMDNSSuffix\": \"...\",\n  \"containerRegistryDNSSuffix\": \"...\",\n  \"cosmosDBDNSSuffix\": \"...\",\n  \"tokenAudience\": \"...\",\n  \"resourceIdentifiers\": {\n    \"graph\": \"...\",\n    \"keyVault\": \"...\",\n    \"datalake\": \"...\",\n    \"batch\": \"...\",\n    \"operationalInsights\": \"...\"\n  }\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThe full list of existing settings for the&#96;AzureChinaCloud&#96;,&#96;AzureGermanCloud&#96;,&#96;AzurePublicCloud&#96; and&#96;AzureUSGovernmentCloud&#96; is available in the source code at [https://github.com/Azure/go-autorest/blob/master/autorest/azure/environments.go#L51](https://github.com/Azure/go-autorest/blob/master/autorest/azure/environments.go#L51).\n\n## Host Network Resources in different AAD Tenant and Subscription\n\nSince v1.18.0, Azure cloud provider supports hosting network resources (Virtual Network, Network Security Group, Route Table, Load Balancer and Public IP) in different AAD Tenant and Subscription than those for the cluster. To enable this feature, set&#96;networkResourceTenantID&#96; and&#96;networkResourceSubscriptionID&#96; in auth config. Note that the value of them need to be different than value of&#96;tenantID&#96; and&#96;subscriptionID&#96;.\n\nWith this feature enabled, network resources of the cluster will be created in&#96;networkResourceSubscriptionID&#96; in&#96;networkResourceTenantID&#96;, and rest resources of the cluster still remain in&#96;subscriptionID&#96; in&#96;tenantID&#96;. Properties which specify the resource groups of network resources are compatible with this feature. For example, Virtual Network will be created in&#96;vnetResourceGroup&#96; in&#96;networkResourceSubscriptionID&#96; in&#96;networkResourceTenantID&#96;.\n\nFor authentication methods, only Service Principal supports this feature, and&#96;aadClientID&#96; and&#96;aadClientSecret&#96; are used to authenticate with those two AAD Tenants and Subscriptions. Managed Identity and Client Certificate doesn’t support this feature. Azure Stack doesn’t support this feature.\n\n## Current default rate-limiting values\n\nThe following are the default rate limiting values configured in [AKS](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/services/kubernetes-service/) and [cluster-api-provider-azure](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api-provider-azure) clusters prior to Kubernetes version v1.18.0.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n    \"cloudProviderBackoff\": true,\n    \"cloudProviderBackoffRetries\": 6,\n    \"cloudProviderBackoffDuration\": 5,\n    \"cloudProviderRatelimit\": true,\n    \"cloudProviderRateLimitQPS\": 10,\n    \"cloudProviderRateLimitBucket\": 100,\n    \"cloudProviderRatelimitQPSWrite\": 10,\n    \"cloudProviderRatelimitBucketWrite\": 100,\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nFor v1.18.0+ refer to [per client rate limit config](https://cloud-provider-azure.sigs.k8s.io/install/configs/#per-client-rate-limiting)\n\n## Feedback\n\nWas this page helpful?\n\nYes No\n\nGlad to hear it! Please [tell us how we can improve](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cloud-provider-azure/issues/new).\n\nSorry to hear that. Please [tell us how we can improve](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cloud-provider-azure/issues/new).\n\nLast modified January 16, 2024: [doc: update documents for DisableAzureStackCloud (9a63a5e73)](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cloud-provider-azure/commit/9a63a5e7344e774b730a46de615d2ea14cb2a485)",
  "url": "https://cloud-provider-azure.sigs.k8s.io/install/configs"
}
```

[Valkey · Valkey Helm: The new way to deploy Valkey on Kubernetes](https://valkey.io/blog/valkey-helm-chart/)\n3. [Initial Release · Issue #11 · valkey-io/valkey-helm](https://github.com/valkey-io/valkey-helm/issues/11)\n4. [valkey-io/valkey-helm](https://github.com/valkey-io/valkey-helm)\n5. [Bitnami Secure Images Helm chart for Valkey](https://techdocs.broadcom.com/us/en/vmware-tanzu/application-catalog/tanzu-application-catalog/services/tac-app-doc/apps-charts-valkey-index.html)\n\n# Valkey Helm Chart: Standalone Single Node Persistence Configuration\n\n## Official Chart Overview\n\nThe official Valkey Helm chart (maintained by valkey-io) supports standalone single-instance deployments with configurable persistence.[1][4] The chart is actively maintained with the latest release being valkey-0.9.3 (January 2026).[4]\n\n## Standalone Deployment\n\nFor a simple single-node setup, the chart deploys Valkey as a single instance, ideal for development and caching workloads.[1] The standalone architecture uses a single pod deployment with an init container that builds configuration at startup.[3]\n\n## Persistence Configuration\n\nThe chart supports **configurable persistent volumes** for data persistence.[1] The persistence parameters in &#96;values.yaml&#96; have been standardized with the following naming conventions:[3]\n\n- **Renamed to \"persistence\"** (previously \"dataStorage\")\n- **Size parameter** (replaces \"requestedSize\")\n- **existingClaim** (for using existing PersistentVolumeClaims)\n\nYou can deploy a single Valkey instance **with or without data persistence** depending on your needs.[2]\n\n## Quick Start\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\nhelm repo add valkey https://valkey.io/valkey-helm/\nhelm install valkey valkey/valkey\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nTo enable persistence and authentication, configure the &#96;values.yaml&#96; accordingly or use Helm flags to override defaults.[1]\n\n## Key Features for Standalone\n\nThe chart supports authentication, configurable persistent volumes, SecurityContext, and RBAC for production deployments.[1] Kubernetes 1.20+ and Helm 3.5+ are required.[4]",

[Unexpected server behaviour with &#96;--disable-cloud-controller&#96; flag · Issue #6554 · k3s-io/k3s](https://github.com/k3s-io/k3s/issues/6554)\n3. [Networking Services | K3s](https://docs.k3s.io/networking/networking-services)\n4. [server | K3s](https://docs.k3s.io/cli/server)\n5. [Managing Packaged Components | K3s](https://docs.k3s.io/installation/packaged-components)\n\n# K3s: Disabling ServiceLB, Traefik, and Cloud Controller Manager\n\n## Disabling Flags\n\nTo disable these components in K3s, use the following flags:[1][3][5]\n\n- &#96;--disable=traefik&#96; - Disables the Traefik ingress controller\n- &#96;--disable=servicelb&#96; - Disables the ServiceLB load balancer controller\n- &#96;--disable-cloud-controller&#96; - Disables the cloud controller manager\n\n**Important:** Use &#96;--disable=traefik&#96; (with equals sign) rather than &#96;--disable traefik&#96; for proper parsing.[1]\n\n## Critical Interaction Between Flags\n\nThere is a critical dependency: &#96;--disable-cloud-controller&#96; only functions properly when &#96;--disable=servicelb&#96; is also supplied.[2] Without disabling ServiceLB, the cloud controller manager will still bind to port 10258, preventing external cloud controller managers from starting.[2] This was identified as a bug in K3s versions 1.25.3-1.25.4.[2]\n\n## Configuration Requirements\n\nBoth &#96;--disable=servicelb&#96; and &#96;--disable-cloud-controller&#96; are listed as critical configuration values that must be set identically across all servers in a cluster.[4] Failure to maintain consistency can cause servers to fail joining the cluster or cause incorrect cluster operation.[4]\n\n## Packaged Components\n\nServiceLB, Traefik, CoreDNS, local-storage, and metrics-server are packaged components deployed as AddOns.[5] When disabled with &#96;--disable&#96; flags, these components are actively uninstalled from the cluster and their manifest files are deleted.[5]\n\n## Azure Considerations\n\nThe search results do not contain specific information about Azure cloud-controller-manager or CSI considerations when using these K3s flags.",

[README.md at 241f9580 · kubernetes-sigs/blob-csi-driver](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/blob-csi-driver/blob/241f9580/README.md)\n3. [kubernetes-sigs/azuredisk-csi-driver](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/azuredisk-csi-driver)\n4. [Setting up the Azure Cloud Provider | Rancher](https://ranchermanager.docs.rancher.com/how-to-guides/new-user-guides/kubernetes-clusters-in-rancher-setup/set-up-cloud-providers/azure)\n5. [Deploy AzureDisk CSI Driver](https://cloud-provider-azure.sigs.k8s.io/install/azuredisk/)\n\n# Azure Disk CSI Driver Setup for K3s Self-Managed Clusters\n\n## Cloud Provider Configuration\n\nTo deploy the Azure Disk CSI driver on a non-AKS self-managed cluster, you need to configure a cloud provider config file (typically &#96;azure.json&#96;).[1] The driver depends on this cloud provider config file containing Azure credentials.[3]\n\n### Config File Location\n- Standard path: &#96;/etc/kubernetes/azure.json&#96;[3]\n- Alternative path: &#96;/etc/kubernetes/cloud.conf&#96; (for Azure Red Hat OpenShift)[3]\n\n### Required Configuration Fields[1]\nThe &#96;azure.json&#96; config file must include:\n- **tenantId** - AAD Tenant ID\n- **aadClientId** - Service principal Client ID\n- **aadClientSecret** - Service principal Client Secret\n- **subscriptionId** - Azure Subscription ID\n- **resourceGroup** - Resource group name\n- **location** - Azure region\n- **useManagedIdentityExtension** - Boolean (default false)\n- Network configuration: vnetName, subnetName, securityGroupName, etc.\n\n### Authentication Methods[1]\nThe cloud provider supports three authentication approaches (priority order):\n1. Managed Identity (system-assigned or user-assigned)\n2. Service Principal (using aadClientId/aadClientSecret)\n3. Client Certificate (using aadClientCertPath/aadClientCertPassword)\n\n## Alternative Configuration: Using Secrets\n\nBoth the Azure Disk and Azure Blob CSI drivers support specifying cloud provider config via Kubernetes secrets instead of files, which may be useful for kubelet configuration in self-managed clusters.[2][3]\n\n## Prerequisites\n\nThe identity used by the driver must have &#96;Contributor&#96; role on both the node resource group and virtual network resource group.[3]\n\n**Note:** The search results focus on general Azure CSI driver setup but do not provide K3s-specific configuration details for kubelet credential providers or secret management.",
      "title": "Web search results for query: azure disk CSI driver k3s \"azure.json\" OR \"azure-cloud-provider\" secret kubelet non-AKS self-managed cluster setup guide"
    }
  ],
  "rejected": false
}

{
  "markdown": "How to Configure Azure Disk CSI on Talos Linux \n\n \n\n \n\n \n\n \n\n \n\n# How to Configure Azure Disk CSI on Talos Linux\n\nStep-by-step guide to deploying the Azure Disk CSI driver on Talos Linux for persistent block storage in Kubernetes.\n\n[ @nawazdhandala](https://github.com/nawazdhandala)• Mar 03, 2026 •\n\n[Talos Linux](https://oneuptime.com/blog/tag/talos-linux) [Azure](https://oneuptime.com/blog/tag/azure) [CSI](https://oneuptime.com/blog/tag/csi) [Storage](https://oneuptime.com/blog/tag/storage) [Kubernetes](https://oneuptime.com/blog/tag/kubernetes) [Azure Disk](https://oneuptime.com/blog/tag/azure-disk)\n\n \n\n \n\n## On this page\n\n \n\n---\n\nStateful workloads on Kubernetes need persistent storage, and if your cluster runs on Azure, Azure Managed Disks are the natural choice for block storage. The Azure Disk CSI driver replaces the legacy in-tree volume plugin and gives you access to newer features like disk snapshots, volume cloning, and Ultra Disk support. This guide covers deploying and configuring the driver on Talos Linux.\n\n## Why Use the CSI Driver Instead of In-Tree\n\nThe in-tree Azure Disk plugin that shipped with older versions of Kubernetes is being deprecated. It was baked into the Kubernetes controller manager and kubelet, which made updates slow and tied storage fixes to Kubernetes release cycles. The CSI driver runs as standalone pods in your cluster and can be updated independently.\n\nBeyond the operational benefits, the CSI driver supports features the in-tree plugin never had: volume snapshots, volume cloning, Ultra Disk and Premium SSD v2 support, shared disks, and on-demand bursting configuration.\n\n## Prerequisites\n\nBefore you begin, ensure you have:\n\n- A Talos Linux cluster running on Azure\n- The Azure cloud provider configured and running\n- &#96;kubectl&#96; and Helm installed\n- A service principal or managed identity with disk management permissions\n\n## IAM Permissions\n\nThe CSI driver needs permissions to create and manage Azure Managed Disks. If you are using a service principal, ensure it has the following role assignment:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n# Assign the Contributor role (or a custom role) to the service principal\n\naz role assignment create \\\n  --assignee \u003cservice-principal-app-id\u003e \\\n  --role \"Contributor\" \\\n  --scope /subscriptions/\u003csubscription-id\u003e/resourceGroups/\u003cresource-group\u003e\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nFor a more restrictive custom role, the driver needs these actions at minimum:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;json\n{\n  \"actions\": [\n    \"Microsoft.Compute/disks/read\",\n    \"Microsoft.Compute/disks/write\",\n    \"Microsoft.Compute/disks/delete\",\n    \"Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/read\",\n    \"Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/write\",\n    \"Microsoft.Compute/snapshots/read\",\n    \"Microsoft.Compute/snapshots/write\",\n    \"Microsoft.Compute/snapshots/delete\"\n  ]\n}\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Deploying the Azure Disk CSI Driver\n\nThe recommended installation method is Helm:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n# Add the Azure Disk CSI Helm repository\nhelm repo add azuredisk-csi-driver https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes-sigs/azuredisk-csi-driver/master/charts\nhelm repo update\n\n# Install the driver\nhelm install azuredisk-csi-driver azuredisk-csi-driver/azuredisk-csi-driver \\\n  --namespace kube-system \\\n  --set controller.replicas=2 \\\n  --set cloud=AzurePublicCloud \\\n  --set linux.distro=fedora\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThe&#96;linux.distro=fedora&#96; setting matters because Talos uses a non-standard filesystem layout. Setting it to&#96;fedora&#96; avoids issues with mount path detection.\n\n## Verifying the Installation\n\nAfter deployment, verify the driver is running:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n# Check that controller and node pods are running\nkubectl get pods -n kube-system -l app=csi-azuredisk-controller\nkubectl get pods -n kube-system -l app=csi-azuredisk-node\n\n# Verify CSI driver registration\nkubectl get csidrivers | grep disk.csi.azure.com\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nYou should see controller pods running on control plane nodes (or wherever they get scheduled) and node pods running as a DaemonSet on every worker node.\n\n## Creating Storage Classes\n\nCreate StorageClasses for different performance tiers:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\n# standard-ssd.yaml - Standard SSD (most common)\napiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1\nkind: StorageClass\nmetadata:\n  name: azure-standard-ssd\n  annotations:\n    storageclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class: \"true\"\nprovisioner: disk.csi.azure.com\nparameters:\n  skuName: StandardSSD_LRS\n  # LRS = Locally Redundant Storage\nreclaimPolicy: Delete\nvolumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer\nallowVolumeExpansion: true\n---\n# premium-ssd.yaml - Premium SSD for production databases\napiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1\nkind: StorageClass\nmetadata:\n  name: azure-premium-ssd\nprovisioner: disk.csi.azure.com\nparameters:\n  skuName: Premium_LRS\n  cachingMode: ReadOnly\nreclaimPolicy: Retain\nvolumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer\nallowVolumeExpansion: true\n---\n# ultra-disk.yaml - Ultra Disk for extreme IOPS\napiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1\nkind: StorageClass\nmetadata:\n  name: azure-ultra-disk\nprovisioner: disk.csi.azure.com\nparameters:\n  skuName: UltraSSD_LRS\n  # Configure IOPS and throughput per volume\n  diskIOPSReadWrite: \"4000\"\n  diskMBpsReadWrite: \"125\"\n  cachingMode: None\n  logicalSectorSize: \"512\"\nreclaimPolicy: Delete\nvolumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n# Apply all storage classes\nkubectl apply -f standard-ssd.yaml\nkubectl apply -f premium-ssd.yaml\nkubectl apply -f ultra-disk.yaml\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThe&#96;WaitForFirstConsumer&#96; binding mode is important. It delays volume creation until a pod actually needs the volume, ensuring the disk is created in the same availability zone as the pod.\n\n## Testing with a Persistent Volume Claim\n\nCreate a PVC and a pod to validate the setup:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\n# test-pvc.yaml\napiVersion: v1\nkind: PersistentVolumeClaim\nmetadata:\n  name: test-azure-disk\nspec:\n  accessModes:\n    - ReadWriteOnce\n  storageClassName: azure-standard-ssd\n  resources:\n    requests:\n      storage: 20Gi\n---\napiVersion: v1\nkind: Pod\nmetadata:\n  name: test-disk-pod\nspec:\n  containers:\n    - name: app\n      image: busybox\n      command: [\"sh\", \"-c\", \"echo 'Azure disk works' \u003e /data/test.txt \u0026\u0026 sleep 3600\"]\n      volumeMounts:\n        - mountPath: /data\n          name: disk-volume\n  volumes:\n    - name: disk-volume\n      persistentVolumeClaim:\n        claimName: test-azure-disk\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n# Deploy and verify\nkubectl apply -f test-pvc.yaml\nkubectl get pvc test-azure-disk --watch\n\n# Once bound, verify the pod\nkubectl exec test-disk-pod -- cat /data/test.txt\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Volume Snapshots\n\nThe Azure Disk CSI driver supports volume snapshots. First, install the snapshot CRDs and controller if not already present:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n# Install snapshot CRDs\nkubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes-csi/external-snapshotter/master/client/config/crd/snapshot.storage.k8s.io_volumesnapshots.yaml\nkubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes-csi/external-snapshotter/master/client/config/crd/snapshot.storage.k8s.io_volumesnapshotcontents.yaml\nkubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes-csi/external-snapshotter/master/client/config/crd/snapshot.storage.k8s.io_volumesnapshotclasses.yaml\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nCreate a VolumeSnapshotClass and take a snapshot:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\n# snapshot-class.yaml\napiVersion: snapshot.storage.k8s.io/v1\nkind: VolumeSnapshotClass\nmetadata:\n  name: azure-disk-snapshot\ndriver: disk.csi.azure.com\ndeletionPolicy: Delete\nparameters:\n  incremental: \"true\"\n---\n# snapshot.yaml\napiVersion: snapshot.storage.k8s.io/v1\nkind: VolumeSnapshot\nmetadata:\n  name: test-disk-snapshot\nspec:\n  volumeSnapshotClassName: azure-disk-snapshot\n  source:\n    persistentVolumeClaimName: test-azure-disk\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nSetting&#96;incremental: \u0026quot;true\u0026quot;&#96; creates incremental snapshots, which are much cheaper and faster than full snapshots.\n\n## Volume Expansion\n\nIf you need more space, expand a PVC by editing the storage request:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n# Expand the volume from 20Gi to 50Gi\nkubectl patch pvc test-azure-disk -p '{\"spec\":{\"resources\":{\"requests\":{\"storage\":\"50Gi\"}}}}'\n\n# Check the status\nkubectl get pvc test-azure-disk\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThe CSI driver handles the expansion automatically. For Standard and Premium SSDs, the expansion happens online without needing to restart the pod.\n\n## Caching and Performance Tuning\n\nAzure Disk caching can significantly impact performance. The&#96;cachingMode&#96; parameter in your StorageClass controls this:\n\n- &#96;None&#96;- No caching, best for write-heavy workloads\n- &#96;ReadOnly&#96;- Caches reads in the host memory, best for read-heavy workloads\n- &#96;ReadWrite&#96;- Caches both reads and writes, best for general workloads (not recommended for databases)\n\nFor databases,&#96;ReadOnly&#96; caching on Premium SSD is usually the best balance of performance and data safety.\n\n## Troubleshooting\n\nIf PVCs are stuck in Pending state, check the CSI controller logs:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n# Check controller logs for provisioning errors\nkubectl logs -n kube-system -l app=csi-azuredisk-controller -c azuredisk --tail=50\n\n# Check events on the PVC\nkubectl describe pvc test-azure-disk\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nCommon issues include insufficient permissions on the service principal, reaching the disk count limit per VM, and availability zone mismatches. Each Azure VM size has a maximum number of data disks it can support, so verify your VM size supports the number of volumes you need.\n\n## Conclusion\n\nThe Azure Disk CSI driver on Talos Linux provides robust persistent storage with support for multiple disk types, snapshots, cloning, and online expansion. Start with Standard SSD for general workloads, Premium SSD for production databases, and Ultra Disk when you need extreme IOPS. The key configuration decisions are StorageClass parameters and the volume binding mode, both of which directly impact performance and reliability.\n\n \n\nShare this article\n\n \n\n \n\n### Nawaz Dhandala\n\nAuthor\n\n@nawazdhandala • Mar 03, 2026 •\n\nNawaz is building OneUptime with a passion for engineering reliable systems and improving observability.\n\n[GitHub](https://github.com/nawazdhandala)\n\n### Improve this Blog Post\n\nAll our blog posts are open source. Found a typo, want to add more detail, or have a better explanation? Anyone can contribute and make this post better for everyone.\n\n[Edit this Post on GitHub](https://github.com/oneuptime/blog/tree/master/posts/2026-03-03-configure-azure-disk-csi-on-talos-linux) [Contributing Guidelines](https://github.com/oneuptime/blog)\n\n \n\n [Open source](https://github.com/oneuptime/oneuptime) \n\n## OneUptime is the Open-Source Observability Platform\n\n \n\nYour complete reliability stack unified: infrastructure monitoring, incident management, status pages, and APM. Open-source and self-hostable.\n\n \n\n[Get started for free](https://oneuptime.com/accounts/register) [Request a demo](https://oneuptime.com/enterprise/demo)\n\n \n\n \n\n[ Status PageReal-time status updates](https://oneuptime.com/product/status-page) \n\n[ IncidentsDetect and resolve fast](https://oneuptime.com/product/incident-management) \n\n[ MonitoringMonitor any resource](https://oneuptime.com/product/monitoring) \n\n[ On-CallSmart alert routing](https://oneuptime.com/product/on-call) \n\n[ MaintenancePlan \u0026 communicate downtime](https://oneuptime.com/product/scheduled-maintenance) \n\n[ LogsFastest log ingest and search](https://oneuptime.com/product/logs-management) \n\n[ MetricsPerformance insights](https://oneuptime.com/product/metrics) \n\n[ TracesEnd-to-end distributed tracing](https://oneuptime.com/product/traces) \n\n[ ExceptionsCatch and fix bugs early](https://oneuptime.com/product/exceptions) \n\n[ WorkflowsAutomate any process](https://oneuptime.com/product/workflows) \n\n[ DashboardsVisualize all your data](https://oneuptime.com/product/dashboards) \n\n[ KubernetesMonitor K8s clusters](https://oneuptime.com/product/kubernetes) \n\n[ ProfilesCPU \u0026 memory profiling](https://oneuptime.com/product/profiles)\n\n \n\n[ AI Agent Automatically detect, diagnose, and resolve incidents with AI-powered root cause analysis and code fixes.](https://oneuptime.com/product/ai-agent)\n\n",
  "url": "https://oneuptime.com/blog/post/2026-03-03-configure-azure-disk-csi-on-talos-linux/view"

The chart supports standalone Valkey deployments using a Deployment controller.[1] \n\nAccording to early design discussions, there were plans to rename persistence-related fields for consistency with standard Helm chart conventions:[1]\n- &#96;dataStorage&#96; was to be renamed to &#96;persistence&#96; or &#96;persistentVolume&#96;\n- &#96;persistentVolumeClaimName&#96; was to be renamed to &#96;existingClaim&#96;\n- &#96;requestedSize&#96; was to be renamed to &#96;size&#96;\n\nHowever, to see the actual current &#96;values.yaml&#96; structure with the specific storageClass configuration for standalone persistence, you would need to view the file directly in the repository at &#96;https://github.com/valkey-io/valkey-helm/blob/main/valkey/values.yaml&#96;.",

{
  "markdown": "global:\n # The global image registry (this will override the registry of all container images defined in this chart)\n imageRegistry: \"\"\n # The global image pull secrets (list of secret names)\n imagePullSecrets: []\n\nimage:\n # Image registry\n registry: \"docker.io\"\n # Valkey container image repository\n repository: valkey/valkey\n # Image pull policy (Always, IfNotPresent, Never)\n pullPolicy: IfNotPresent\n # Image tag (leave empty to use .Chart.AppVersion)\n tag: \"\"\n\n# List of image pull secrets (for private registries)\nimagePullSecrets: []\n\n# Override the default name or full name of resources\nnameOverride: \"\"\nfullnameOverride: \"\"\n\n# Kubernetes cluster domain name\nclusterDomain: cluster.local\n\nserviceAccount:\n # Create a service account for Valkey\n create: true\n # Whether to automount the service account token\n automount: false\n # Annotations to add to the service account\n annotations: {}\n # Name of an existing service account to use (if create: false)\n name: \"\"\n\n# Annotations and labels for the pods\npodAnnotations: {}\npodLabels: {}\n\n# Annotations for the Deployment/StatefulSet resource\nworkloadAnnotations: {}\n\n# Common labels to add to all resources (Deployment, Service, ConfigMap, etc.)\ncommonLabels: {}\n\n# Security context for the pod (applies to all containers)\npodSecurityContext:\n fsGroup: 1000\n runAsUser: 1000\n runAsGroup: 1000\n seccompProfile:\n type: RuntimeDefault\n\n# Priority class name for pod scheduling (leave empty to use cluster's default)\npriorityClassName: \"\"\n\n# Security context for the Valkey containers\nsecurityContext:\n allowPrivilegeEscalation: false\n capabilities:\n drop:\n - ALL\n readOnlyRootFilesystem: true\n runAsNonRoot: true\n runAsUser: 1000\n\nservice:\n # Type of Kubernetes service (ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer)\n type: ClusterIP\n # Port on which Valkey will be exposed\n port: 6379\n annotations: {}\n # NodePort value (if service.type is NodePort)\n nodePort: 0\n # ClusterIP value\n clusterIP: \"\"\n # Class of a load balancer implementation\n loadBalancerClass: \"\"\n # If specified and supported by the platform, this will restrict traffic through the cloud-provider load-balancer\n # will be restricted to the specified client IPs.\n # This field will be ignored if the cloud-provider does not support the feature.\n loadBalancerSourceRanges: []\n # Application protocol\n appProtocol: \"\"\n\n# Network policy to control traffic to the pods\n# More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/network-policies/\nnetworkPolicy: {}\n\n# Resource limits/requests for the main Valkey container\nresources: {}\n # Example:\n # limits:\n # cpu: 100m\n # memory: 128Mi\n # requests:\n # cpu: 100m\n # memory: 128Mi\n\n# Resource limits/requests for init containers\ninitResources: {}\n # Example:\n # limits:\n # cpu: 100m\n # memory: 128Mi\n # requests:\n # cpu: 100m\n # memory: 128Mi\n\n# Additional init containers\nextraInitContainers: []\n\n# Additional containers\nextraContainers: []\n\n# Persistent storage configuration (standalone deployment only)\ndataStorage:\n # Enable persistent volume claim creation\n enabled: false\n\n # Use existing PVC by name (skip dynamic provisioning if set)\n persistentVolumeClaimName: \"\"\n\n # Subpath inside PVC to mount\n subPath: \"\"\n\n # Name of the volume (referenced in deployment)\n volumeName: \"valkey-data\"\n\n # Request size (e.g. 5Gi) for dynamically provisioned volume\n requestedSize: \"\"\n\n # Name of the storage class to use\n className: \"\"\n\n # Access modes for the PVC (e.g., ReadWriteOnce, ReadWriteMany)\n accessModes:\n - ReadWriteOnce\n\n # If true, keep the PVC on Helm uninstall\n keepPvc: false\n\n # Optional annotations to add to the PVC\n annotations: {}\n\n # Optional labels to add to the PVC\n labels: {}\n\n # If hostPath is set then a hostPath DirectoryOrCreate volume is used\n hostPath: \"\"\n\n# Mount additional secrets into the Valkey container\nextraValkeySecrets: []\n\n# Mount additional configMaps into the Valkey container\nextraValkeyConfigs: []\n\n# Mount extra secrets as volume to init container (deprecated, use extraValkeySecrets)\nextraSecretValkeyConfigs: false\n\n# Mount additional emptyDir or hostPath volumes (advanced use)\nextraVolumes: []\n# - name: hostpath-volume\n# hostPath:\n# path: /opt/valkey/hostpath-volume/\n# type: DirectoryOrCreate\nextraVolumeMounts: []\n# - mountPath: /some/path/\n# name: hostpath-volume\n\n# Content for valkey.conf (will be mounted via ConfigMap)\nvalkeyConfig: \"\"\n\nauth:\n # Enable ACL-based authentication\n # IMPORTANT: When authentication is enabled, the 'default' user MUST be defined in either\n # aclUsers or aclConfig. Without a default user, anyone can access the database without\n # credentials, creating a security risk.\n enabled: false\n\n # Use an existing secret for user passwords. Key defaults to username.\n usersExistingSecret: \"\"\n\n # Map of users to create with ACL permissions.\n # If usersExistingSecret is set, passwords from the secret take priority over inline passwords.\n # NOTE: If using aclUsers, the 'default' user must be included here.\n aclUsers: {}\n # Example:\n # default:\n # permissions: \"~* \u0026* +@all\"\n # password: \"secretpass\" # Inline password (fallback if usersExistingSecret not set)\n # passwordKey: \"admin-pwd\" # Key in usersExistingSecret (defaults to username)\n # read-user:\n # permissions: \"~* -@all +@read +ping +info\"\n\n # Inline ACL configuration that will be appended after generated users.\n # NOTE: If using aclConfig, ensure the 'default' user is defined here.\n aclConfig: \"\"\n # Example:\n # aclConfig: |\n # user default on \u003esecretpass ~* \u0026* +@all\n\n# Replica configuration for master-replica replication mode\nreplica:\n enabled: false\n\n # Number of replica instances (total pods = replicas + 1 master)\n replicas: 2\n\n # Username for replicas to authenticate to master, ignored if auth.enabled is false.\n # IMPORTANT: When auth.enabled is true, this user MUST be defined in auth.aclUsers.\n # The chart requires this to retrieve the password for replica authentication.\n # The user must have appropriate replication permissions: +psync +replconf +ping\n replicationUser: \"default\"\n\n # Replication settings\n # Use diskless replication (sync directly from memory) vs disk-based\n disklessSync: false\n\n # Write safety - require minimum number of healthy replicas to accept writes\n # Set to 0 to disable this check, or 1+ to require minimum replicas before accepting writes\n # This ensures data durability by requiring at least N replicas to be in sync\n minReplicasToWrite: 0\n\n # Maximum replication lag in seconds before a replica is considered unhealthy\n minReplicasMaxLag: 10\n\n # Read service configuration\n service:\n # Enable read service (load balances read traffic across all pods)\n enabled: true\n # Service type (ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer)\n type: ClusterIP\n # Port on which the read service will be exposed\n port: 6379\n # Optional annotations for the read service\n annotations: {}\n # NodePort value (if service.type is NodePort)\n nodePort: 0\n # ClusterIP value\n clusterIP: \"\"\n # Application protocol\n appProtocol: \"\"\n # Class of a load balancer implementation\n loadBalancerClass: \"\"\n # If specified and supported by the platform, this will restrict traffic through the cloud-provider load-balancer\n # will be restricted to the specified client IPs.\n # This field will be ignored if the cloud-provider does not support the feature.\n loadBalancerSourceRanges: []\n\n # Persistence configuration (required for replicas)\n persistence:\n # Size of the PVC for each replica (required when replica.enabled is true)\n size: \"\"\n # Storage class name (empty = use default storage class)\n storageClass: \"\"\n # Access modes for the PVC\n accessModes:\n - ReadWriteOnce\n\n # PersistentVolumeClaim retention policy for StatefulSet\n # Controls when PVCs are deleted (requires Kubernetes 1.23+)\n # More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/statefulset/#persistentvolumeclaim-retention\n persistentVolumeClaimRetentionPolicy: {}\n\ntls:\n # Enable TLS\n enabled: false\n # Name of the Secret containing TLS keys (required)\n existingSecret: \"\"\n # Secret key name containing server public certificate\n serverPublicKey: server.crt\n # Secret key name containing server private key\n serverKey: server.key\n # Secret key name containing Certificate Authority public certificate\n caPublicKey: ca.crt\n # Secret key name containing DH parameters (optional)\n dhParamKey: \"\"\n # Require that clients authenticate with a certificate\n requireClientCertificate: false\n\n# Node selector for pod assignment\nnodeSelector: {}\n\n# Tolerations for pod assignment to tainted nodes\ntolerations: []\n\n# Affinity rules for pod scheduling\naffinity: {}\n\n# Set Deployment strategy. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/deployment/#strategy\ndeploymentStrategy: RollingUpdate # @schema enum:[RollingUpdate,Recreate]\n\n# See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/scheduling-eviction/topology-spread-constraints\ntopologySpreadConstraints: []\n\n# PodDisruptionBudget configuration (only used in replicated mode)\n# Helps keep enough replicas available during voluntary disruptions\npodDisruptionBudget:\n # Enable PodDisruptionBudget\n enabled: false\n # Minimum number of pods that must be available (takes precedence over maxUnavailable if both set)\n minAvailable: null\n # Maximum number of pods that can be unavailable during disruptions\n maxUnavailable: 1\n # Unhealthy pod eviction policy (optional)\n # Valid values: IfHealthyBudget, AlwaysAllow\n unhealthyPodEvictionPolicy: \"\"\n\n# Valkey logging level: debug, verbose, notice, warning\nvalkeyLogLevel: \"notice\"\n# Environment variables to inject into Valkey container\n\nenv: {}\n # Example:\n # LOG_LEVEL: info\n\nmetrics:\n # Enable Prometheus exporter sidecar\n enabled: false\n # Exporter configuration\n exporter:\n # Command to run in the metrics exporter container (overrides args)\n command: []\n # Here is a comprehensive summary of my findings across all your questions.

---

## 1. Azure Disk CSI Driver Helm Chart Installation

**Repository:** [kubernetes-sigs/azuredisk-csi-driver](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/azuredisk-csi-driver) (latest: v1.34.2, supports Kubernetes 1.21+)

```bash
helm repo add azuredisk-csi-driver https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes-sigs/azuredisk-csi-driver/master/charts
helm repo update

helm install azuredisk-csi-driver azuredisk-csi-driver/azuredisk-csi-driver \
  --namespace kube-system \
  --set controller.replicas=1 \
  --set cloud=AzurePublicCloud
```

Verify with:

```bash
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l app=csi-azuredisk-controller
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l app=csi-azuredisk-node
kubectl get csidrivers | grep disk.csi.azure.com
```

**Important caveat:** Deploying this driver manually outside AKS is **not an officially supported Microsoft product**. It works, but you're on your own for support.

---

## 2. Azure Credentials / Identity

The CSI driver reads credentials from a **cloud provider config file** (`/etc/kubernetes/azure.json`) or from a **Kubernetes Secret** named `azure-cloud-provider` in `kube-system` (key: `cloud-config`).

### Minimum `azure.json` for Service Principal auth:

```json
{
  "cloud": "AzurePublicCloud",
  "tenantId": "<AAD_TENANT_ID>",
  "aadClientId": "<SERVICE_PRINCIPAL_APP_ID>",
  "aadClientSecret": "<SERVICE_PRINCIPAL_SECRET>",
  "subscriptionId": "<SUBSCRIPTION_ID>",
  "resourceGroup": "<NODE_RESOURCE_GROUP>",
  "location": "<AZURE_REGION>",
  "vmType": "standard",
  "useManagedIdentityExtension": false,
  "useInstanceMetadata": true
}
```

### Authentication methods (in priority order):
1. **Managed Identity** - set `useManagedIdentityExtension: true` (+ `userAssignedIdentityID` for user-assigned)
2. **Service Principal** - set `aadClientId` + `aadClientSecret`
3. **Client Certificate** - set `aadClientCertPath` + `aadClientCertPassword`

### Required IAM permissions

The identity needs **Contributor** on the resource group (or a custom role with these minimum actions):

```json
{
  "actions": [
    "Microsoft.Compute/disks/read",
    "Microsoft.Compute/disks/write",
    "Microsoft.Compute/disks/delete",
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/read",
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/write",
    "Microsoft.Compute/snapshots/read",
    "Microsoft.Compute/snapshots/write",
    "Microsoft.Compute/snapshots/delete"
  ]
}
```

### Secret-based approach (alternative to file on disk)

Instead of placing `azure.json` on every node, you can create a Kubernetes secret:

```bash
kubectl create secret generic azure-cloud-provider \
  --namespace kube-system \
  --from-file=cloud-config=azure.json
```

The CSI driver can read the config from this secret. You can also point to a custom secret via a ConfigMap named `azure-cred-file`.

Sources: [Cloud Provider Azure Config Docs](https://cloud-provider-azure.sigs.k8s.io/install/configs), [azuredisk-csi-driver GitHub](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/azuredisk-csi-driver)

---

## 3. k3s-Specific Considerations

Several things differ from AKS when running on self-managed k3s:

| Concern | Detail |
|---|---|
| **No built-in cloud provider** | k3s has no Azure cloud provider. You must supply `azure.json` yourself (file on nodes or secret). |
| **`--disable-cloud-controller`** | If you want to run an external Azure cloud-controller-manager, you must pass `--disable-cloud-controller` **and** `--disable=servicelb` to k3s server. Omitting `--disable=servicelb` causes a port conflict on 10258. |
| **Consistency across servers** | Both `--disable=servicelb` and `--disable-cloud-controller` must be set identically on **all** server nodes, or joining will fail. |
| **No IMDS by default** | On AKS, the Azure Instance Metadata Service (IMDS) provides identity. On self-hosted k3s VMs, you need to ensure IMDS is accessible or use service principal auth instead. Set `useInstanceMetadata: true` in `azure.json` if your VMs are Azure VMs with IMDS. |
| **`linux.distro` Helm value** | If your k3s nodes use a non-standard filesystem layout (e.g., Talos, Flatcar), set `--set linux.distro=fedora` to avoid mount path detection issues. For standard distros (Ubuntu, Debian), the default is fine. |
| **No built-in StorageClasses** | AKS ships with `managed-csi` and `managed-csi-premium`. On k3s you must create them yourself (see below). |
| **File placement** | The `azure.json` must exist at `/etc/kubernetes/azure.json` on every node (or be provided via secret). k3s doesn't create this path by default. |

---

## 4. StorageClass for Azure Managed Disks

Create these after the CSI driver is installed:

```yaml
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: managed-csi
  annotations:
    storageclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class: "true"
provisioner: disk.csi.azure.com
parameters:
  skuName: StandardSSD_LRS
reclaimPolicy: Delete
volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer
allowVolumeExpansion: true
---
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: managed-csi-premium
provisioner: disk.csi.azure.com
parameters:
  skuName: Premium_LRS
  cachingMode: ReadOnly
reclaimPolicy: Retain
volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer
allowVolumeExpansion: true
```

Key parameters:

| Parameter | Options |
|---|---|
| `skuName` | `Standard_LRS`, `StandardSSD_LRS`, `Premium_LRS`, `StandardSSD_ZRS`, `Premium_ZRS`, `UltraSSD_LRS` |
| `cachingMode` | `None`, `ReadOnly`, `ReadWrite` (use `None` for Ultra Disks, `ReadOnly` for database workloads on Premium) |
| `volumeBindingMode` | Always use `WaitForFirstConsumer` to ensure the disk is created in the same AZ as the pod |
| Access mode | Only `ReadWriteOnce` is supported for Azure Disks |

Source: [OneUptime Azure Disk CSI on Talos guide (March 2026)](https://oneuptime.com/blog/post/2026-03-03-configure-azure-disk-csi-on-talos-linux/view)

---

## 5. Valkey Helm Chart (Single-Node with Persistence)

**Recommended chart:** The **official Valkey Helm chart** from [valkey-io/valkey-helm](https://github.com/valkey-io/valkey-helm) (latest: `valkey-0.9.3`, Jan 2026). Requires Kubernetes 1.20+ and Helm 3.5+.

### Installation:

```bash
helm repo add valkey https://valkey.io/valkey-helm/
helm repo update

helm install valkey valkey/valkey \
  --namespace valkey \
  --create-namespace \
  --set dataStorage.enabled=true \
  --set dataStorage.requestedSize=10Gi \
  --set dataStorage.className=managed-csi
```

### Key `values.yaml` sections for standalone + persistence:

```yaml
# Standalone mode (replica.enabled defaults to false, so it's single-node by default)
replica:
  enabled: false

# Persistence
dataStorage:
  enabled: true
  requestedSize: "10Gi"
  className: "managed-csi"       # your StorageClass name
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  keepPvc: true                  # keep PVC on helm uninstall

# Optional: authentication
auth:
  enabled: true
  aclUsers:
    default:
      permissions: "~* &* +@all"
      password: "your-password"

# Optional: Prometheus metrics
metrics:
  enabled: true
```

The chart deploys as a **Deployment** (not StatefulSet) in standalone mode, with an init container that builds configuration at startup. The `dataStorage` block creates a PVC for RDB/AOF persistence.

**Why the official chart over Bitnami?** The Valkey project explicitly recommends their own chart as the successor to Bitnami charts, which have faced distribution and licensing changes. The official chart is lighter-weight and purpose-built.

Sources: [Valkey Helm Blog Post](https://valkey.io/blog/valkey-helm-chart/), [valkey-io/valkey-helm GitHub](https://github.com/valkey-io/valkey-helm), [Valkey Helm Charts](https://valkey.io/valkey-helm/)
